stem: A senior engineer on your team has refined an extensive set of coding conventions for your service — commit message format, test fixture patterns, API error-handling rules — and Claude Code follows them perfectly in her sessions. A new teammate clones the repository, starts Claude Code, and none of these conventions are applied: Claude generates code that violates the team's error-handling rules and uses the wrong fixture patterns. You confirm the senior engineer's conventions live in her ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. What is the correct fix?
correct_choice: Move the team conventions into a project-level CLAUDE.md (./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.md) committed to the repository, so every teammate receives them via version control.
correct_rationale: User-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md applies only to that user's own sessions and is never shared through the repository. Team-wide conventions belong at the project level, where they are version-controlled and automatically loaded for anyone who clones the repo — the exact configuration-hierarchy diagnosis the exam guide calls out in task 3.1.
near_miss_choice: Have the new teammate copy the senior engineer's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md into his own home directory so his sessions load the same instructions.
near_miss_rationale: Copying works for one person one time, but it duplicates the conventions outside version control: future edits by the senior engineer never propagate, the copies drift, and every subsequent hire repeats the same manual step. It treats a project-scoping problem as a personal-configuration problem.
distractor1_choice: Add the conventions to .claude/settings.json in the repository, since settings files are shared with the team via version control.
distractor1_rationale: settings.json carries configuration such as permissions, environment variables, and hooks — not natural-language instructions. Conventions written there would not be injected into Claude's context as instructions; CLAUDE.md is the mechanism for persistent project guidance.
distractor2_choice: Instruct the new teammate to paste the conventions into the chat at the start of each session until he has internalized the workflow.
distractor2_rationale: Per-session pasting is exactly the repetition CLAUDE.md exists to eliminate: it is manual, easy to forget, consumes fresh input tokens every session, and leaves the team with no single version-controlled source of truth for the conventions.

stem: Your monorepo's root CLAUDE.md has grown past 600 lines because it inlines the full coding standards for every package: the React frontend, the Go API, and the Terraform infrastructure. Maintainers complain that editing standards means hunting through one giant file, and package owners want to keep their standards docs next to their code in docs/ directories they already maintain. How should you restructure so each package's CLAUDE.md stays small while still loading the relevant standards?
correct_choice: Keep the standards in their existing per-package docs files and reference them from each package's CLAUDE.md with @path import syntax (e.g., @docs/api-standards.md), so the referenced files are expanded into context at launch.
correct_rationale: The @path/to/import syntax exists precisely for this: CLAUDE.md files can import external files, which are expanded and loaded alongside the CLAUDE.md that references them. Maintainers keep editing the docs files they own, each package's CLAUDE.md stays a thin index, and the guide's task 3.1 names @import for selectively including the standards files relevant to each package.
near_miss_choice: Copy each package's standards section out of the root CLAUDE.md and paste it into that package's directory-level CLAUDE.md file.
near_miss_rationale: This fixes the monolith but duplicates content the maintainers already keep in docs/ — now every standards change must be made twice, and the copies will drift apart. Importing the existing docs files avoids maintaining parallel versions of the same rules.
distractor1_choice: Delete the standards from CLAUDE.md entirely and rely on Claude to discover and read the docs/ files on demand when it needs them.
distractor1_rationale: Nothing guarantees Claude reads a given docs file before generating code; conventions load reliably only when CLAUDE.md (or a file it imports) carries them. On-demand discovery turns deterministic instruction loading into a hope.
distractor2_choice: Consolidate all standards into the root CLAUDE.md under clearer per-package headers so Claude can infer which section applies to the files it is editing.
distractor2_rationale: This preserves the monolith the team is trying to escape: every session pays the context cost of all packages' standards regardless of relevance, the file keeps growing, and relying on Claude to infer which section applies is weaker than loading only the applicable standards.

stem: Claude Code reliably follows your database-migration conventions in sessions you start from the repository root, but a teammate reports that in his sessions Claude ignores those same conventions entirely, and behavior seems to vary between his own sessions depending on which subdirectory he launches from. Before rewriting any instructions, what is the most effective first diagnostic step?
correct_choice: Run the /memory command in an affected session to see exactly which CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, and rules files were loaded, and confirm whether the migration conventions file is present.
correct_rationale: /memory lists every memory file loaded in the current session. Inconsistent behavior across users and launch directories is the signature of a loading problem — instructions living in a file that only some sessions pick up — and /memory verifies loading directly instead of guessing. The exam guide names /memory for diagnosing inconsistent behavior across sessions.
near_miss_choice: Ask Claude in the affected session whether it has read the migration conventions and to summarize them, to check what it knows.
near_miss_rationale: Claude's self-report is an unreliable proxy: it may accurately summarize conventions from general knowledge without them being in context, or hedge when they are. /memory gives ground truth about what was actually loaded; asking the model gives a plausible-sounding answer either way.
distractor1_choice: Rewrite the conventions in stronger, more emphatic language (bold text, MUST/ALWAYS) so Claude stops ignoring them.
distractor1_rationale: Emphasis only helps if the instructions are in context at all. If the file isn't loaded in the teammate's sessions, no amount of stronger wording changes anything — this treats a loading problem as an adherence problem before diagnosing which one it is.
distractor2_choice: Have the teammate reinstall Claude Code to reset its configuration state, since behavior differs between machines.
distractor2_rationale: Nothing suggests a corrupted installation; the symptom pattern (varies by user and launch directory) points at which memory files get discovered and loaded. Reinstalling is a shot in the dark that skips the cheap, direct diagnostic.

stem: Before every release, engineers on your team paste the same 30-line release-verification prompt into Claude Code: check migration ordering, confirm feature flags default off, verify changelog entries. You want this available as a /release-check command to every engineer automatically as soon as they clone or pull the repository, with a single maintained definition. Where should the command live?
correct_choice: In the repository's .claude/commands/ directory (e.g., .claude/commands/release-check.md), committed to version control.
correct_rationale: Project-scoped commands in .claude/commands/ are shared through the repository itself: everyone who clones or pulls gets /release-check with zero setup, and there is exactly one definition to maintain. This is the project-vs-user command scoping the exam guide tests in task 3.2.
near_miss_choice: In each engineer's ~/.claude/commands/ directory, distributed once via the team chat channel.
near_miss_rationale: ~/.claude/commands/ is user scope — personal commands that are not version-controlled. Distribution becomes a manual step for every engineer and every future edit, and the copies drift the first time someone updates the checklist. It works, but fails the 'automatically available and singly maintained' requirement.
distractor1_choice: As a section in the project CLAUDE.md titled 'Release checklist' that Claude applies whenever a release is mentioned.
distractor1_rationale: CLAUDE.md content is always-loaded context, so the 30-line checklist would consume tokens in every session including the vast majority that have nothing to do with releases — and triggering on the word 'release' is discretionary rather than an explicit, on-demand /command invocation.
distractor2_choice: In a docs/release-check.md file that engineers are instructed to reference with an @ mention when they need it.
distractor2_rationale: A plain docs file creates no slash command; each use still depends on engineers remembering the ritual and pointing Claude at the right file. The .claude/commands/ mechanism exists precisely to package this as a first-class, discoverable /command.

stem: Your team's /audit-deps skill walks the dependency tree, reads dozens of manifest and lockfile entries, and produces a long vulnerability report. Engineers complain that after running it, the main conversation is so full of dependency listings that Claude starts losing track of the feature work they were in the middle of. You want the skill to keep producing its full report without its intermediate output polluting the main session. Which change accomplishes this?
correct_choice: Add context: fork to the skill's SKILL.md frontmatter so the skill executes in an isolated forked subagent context and only its result returns to the main conversation.
correct_rationale: context: fork is the frontmatter option for running a skill in an isolated sub-agent context: the skill's exploration and verbose intermediate output stay in the fork, and the main session receives the result. This is exactly the 'preventing skill outputs from polluting the main conversation' behavior the exam guide describes in task 3.2.
near_miss_choice: Add allowed-tools frontmatter listing only the tools the audit needs, so the skill runs with a restricted tool set.
near_miss_rationale: allowed-tools governs tool permissions while the skill is active — it does not change where the skill executes or where its output accumulates. The dependency listings would still land in the main conversation's context.
distractor1_choice: Add argument-hint frontmatter so engineers invoke the skill with a narrower target and it produces less output.
distractor1_rationale: argument-hint is an autocomplete affordance that shows users what arguments a skill expects; it neither constrains the skill's output volume nor isolates its context. The report is supposed to be comprehensive — the goal is isolation, not truncation.
distractor2_choice: Document that engineers should run /compact immediately after the skill finishes to shrink the conversation back down.
distractor2_rationale: Reactive compaction is lossy summarization applied after the damage is done — details of the interrupted feature work can be degraded along with the dependency noise. Isolating the verbose work so it never enters the main context is strictly better than cleaning up afterwards.

stem: Your platform has strict conventions for SQL migration files — forward-only, one DDL statement per file, mandatory rollback comment header — and migration files live in per-service directories scattered across the repo (services/billing/migrations/, services/auth/migrations/, and a dozen more, with new services added monthly). You want Claude to apply the migration conventions automatically whenever it edits any migration file, without loading them into sessions that never touch migrations. What is the most maintainable setup?
correct_choice: Create one .claude/rules/migrations.md file with YAML frontmatter paths: ["**/migrations/*.sql"] so the rule loads only when Claude works with matching files, wherever they live.
correct_rationale: Path-scoped rules in .claude/rules/ activate on glob-matched file paths regardless of directory location — one file covers every current and future service, loads only when relevant (saving context in unrelated sessions), and new services inherit the conventions with zero additional setup. This is the glob-pattern-over-directory-files advantage the exam guide tests in task 3.3.
near_miss_choice: Place a CLAUDE.md file inside each service's migrations/ directory containing the migration conventions.
near_miss_rationale: Directory-level CLAUDE.md files do load when Claude works in those directories, but this means maintaining a dozen duplicated copies that drift apart, plus a manual step every time a service is added — the exact multi-directory maintenance burden path-scoped rules eliminate.
distractor1_choice: Add the migration conventions as a section of the root CLAUDE.md so they are guaranteed to be in context for every session.
distractor1_rationale: Root CLAUDE.md loads unconditionally, so every session — frontend work, docs edits, everything — pays the context cost of migration rules it will never use, and the requirement was specifically to avoid loading them when irrelevant.
distractor2_choice: Package the conventions as a skill that Claude loads when it determines migration work is happening.
distractor2_rationale: Skill loading on relevance is discretionary — Claude may or may not pull the skill in before editing a migration file. Conventions that must apply deterministically to matching files are what paths-scoped rules provide; a skill adds an invocation dependency where none is needed.

stem: You have two tasks queued for Claude Code this morning. Task 1: replace the ORM across your API layer — roughly 60 model and repository files, with at least three viable migration strategies that differ in how transactions and lazy loading are handled. Task 2: a bug report with a stack trace showing a missing null check in one utility function. How should you run these two tasks?
correct_choice: Use plan mode for the ORM replacement so Claude explores the codebase and commits to a migration strategy before editing, and direct execution for the null-check fix.
correct_rationale: Plan mode is designed for large-scale, multi-file changes with multiple valid approaches and architectural implications — exploring first prevents costly rework from a strategy chosen blind. A single-file fix with a clear stack trace is exactly the well-scoped change direct execution is for. Matching mode to task complexity is task 3.4 of the exam guide.
near_miss_choice: Use plan mode for both tasks, since planning before execution reduces risk regardless of task size.
near_miss_rationale: For a bounded single-function fix with a stack trace pointing at the line, a planning phase adds latency and ceremony without reducing any real risk — the change has one obvious scope and approach. Plan mode's value is proportional to architectural uncertainty, which the small task lacks.
distractor1_choice: Use direct execution for both tasks, giving the ORM migration comprehensive upfront instructions describing exactly how each service should be restructured.
distractor1_rationale: Comprehensive upfront instructions assume you already know the right migration strategy without Claude having explored the dependency structure — the assumption plan mode exists to avoid. Discovering mid-execution that the chosen strategy mishandles transactions means large-scale rework.
distractor2_choice: Start both in direct execution and switch the ORM task into plan mode only if Claude encounters unexpected complexity.
distractor2_rationale: The complexity is not unexpected — 60 files and three competing strategies are stated up front. Reactive switching means the first stretch of edits happens without a committed strategy, and work done before the switch may follow an approach the plan later abandons.

stem: You ask Claude Code to write a normalizer that converts scraped product dimension strings into structured millimeter values. Your prose spec says to 'handle common formats sensibly,' but across three attempts Claude handles the ambiguous cases differently each time: sometimes '4 x 6 in' becomes width/height, sometimes height/width; "5'" is parsed as feet in one attempt and as a data error in another. The function compiles and passes the happy-path tests every time. What is the most effective way to get consistent, correct handling of these edge cases?
correct_choice: Add 2-3 concrete input/output example pairs to the request covering exactly the ambiguous formats — e.g., '4 x 6 in' → {width_mm: 101.6, height_mm: 152.4} — and ask Claude to regenerate against them.
correct_rationale: When natural-language descriptions are interpreted inconsistently, concrete input/output examples are the most effective way to communicate the expected transformation — they resolve each ambiguity by demonstration rather than by prose that itself needs interpreting. This is the exam guide's task 3.5 iterative-refinement pattern.
near_miss_choice: Expand the prose specification to several paragraphs precisely describing every dimension format and the rule for each.
near_miss_rationale: Longer prose is still prose: each rule is one more sentence to interpret, and exhaustively enumerating formats in words is harder to get right than showing the mapping directly. The observed failure mode — inconsistent interpretation across attempts — is the signature of description-based specs, which more description dilutes rather than fixes.
distractor1_choice: Ask Claude to restate the specification in its own words and confirm it understands before regenerating the function.
distractor1_rationale: A restated spec is a self-report, not a constraint: Claude can paraphrase the prose correctly and still resolve '4 x 6' order differently at generation time, because the ambiguity lives in the spec, not in Claude's reading of it.
distractor2_choice: Generate five candidate implementations and keep the one that handles the most edge cases correctly.
distractor2_rationale: Sampling more candidates from the same ambiguous spec doesn't define what 'correctly' means for the contested cases — you would be picking a winner by a judgment the spec still hasn't pinned down, and the next regeneration starts from the same ambiguity.

stem: Your CI pipeline runs claude -p to review each pull request and must post the findings as inline PR comments, which requires each finding's file path, line number, severity, and description as separate fields. The current implementation asks Claude to 'end your review with a JSON block' and regex-extracts it from the response; roughly one run in ten fails because the JSON is malformed, wrapped in prose, or missing a field. What is the correct fix?
correct_choice: Run claude -p with --output-format json and --json-schema pointing at a findings schema, then read the validated result from the structured_output field of the response.
correct_rationale: --output-format json with --json-schema is the documented mechanism for enforcing schema-conformant structured output from CI-invoked Claude Code: the response carries the findings in a structured_output field that conforms to the supplied JSON Schema, eliminating the parse-from-prose step entirely. This is task 3.6 of the exam guide.
near_miss_choice: Switch to --output-format json alone and parse the findings out of the response's result text field.
near_miss_rationale: --output-format json structures the envelope (result, session ID, metadata) but the result field is still free-form text — the findings inside it remain prose that must be regex-extracted, so the one-in-ten malformed-JSON failure mode survives intact. The --json-schema flag is what constrains the payload itself.
distractor1_choice: Strengthen the prompt to 'Respond with ONLY valid JSON matching this exact format, no other text' and add a retry loop for malformed output.
distractor1_rationale: Prompt-level format demands are probabilistic — this is the current failing approach with louder wording plus retries that add latency and cost. When the CLI offers schema enforcement, relying on instruction-following for machine-critical structure is the anti-pattern.
distractor2_choice: Use --output-format stream-json so each finding arrives as its own newline-delimited JSON event that the pipeline can parse incrementally.
distractor2_rationale: stream-json emits transport events for real-time streaming — message deltas and lifecycle records — not schema-validated findings objects. The pipeline doesn't need incremental delivery; it needs the payload to conform to a schema, which is --json-schema's job.
